---
title: "II — Devices and analog circuits"
description: "From semiconductor behavior to bounded, measured signal chains."
page-role: part
part-id: analog
---

Analog design connects device physics to continuously varying signals. The aim
is not to memorize ideal circuits, but to know which approximation controls a
design decision and which non-ideal effect will invalidate it first.

## Part question

How do physical devices shape, switch, amplify, and measure real signals within
finite voltage, current, noise, bandwidth, and thermal limits?

## Entry knowledge

You should be comfortable with DC networks, power, measurement loading,
transients, and impedance from Part I. Review [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd)
for logarithms and complex quantities and keep [F06](../01-foundations/f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd)
nearby when interpreting device curves or measured gain.

## Learning arc

| Chapters | Development |
|---|---|
| A01–A02 | move from semiconductor behavior to diode and transistor operating regions |
| A03–A04 | create gain and predictable closed-loop behavior through bias and feedback |
| A05–A06 | build signal chains while accounting for calibration, noise, nonlinearity, bandwidth, and stability |
| A07 | connect circuit behavior to integrated implementation and layout-dependent effects |

## Exit capability

You should be able to select an operating point, predict small- and large-signal
behavior, identify the dominant error term, choose a device or amplifier from
bounded requirements, and define measurements that expose saturation,
instability, noise, or thermal failure.

Labs L06–L08 provide the main evidence sequence. The precision-instrument thread
continues through S01, S02, R05, and system study X01; see the
[practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).
